---
title: "Data Report"
---

All information on the data used in the project is documented here to ensure
traceability and reproducibility.

---

## Raw Data

### Overview

: Overview of raw datasets used in the project. {#tbl-raw-overview}

| Name | Source | Storage |
|---|---|---|
| Nikity/Epstein-Files | HuggingFace (pre-extracted from DOJ/House Oversight source documents) | HuggingFace Hub — Parquet format, streamed via the `datasets` library |

### Nikity/Epstein-Files

The dataset aggregates legal documents, court records, emails, and related files from the
Epstein-related releases by the U.S. Department of Justice and the U.S. House Oversight
Committee. Text has been pre-extracted from PDF and image files using OCR.

**Provenance:** Each row contains an `online_url` field linking to the original DOJ-hosted
document, making the provenance of every data point independently verifiable.

**Legal status:** The underlying documents are U.S. Government publications and are in the
public domain. The HuggingFace dataset is a derived work that aggregates and processes
these public records. No personally sensitive data is introduced beyond what is already
part of the publicly released court record.

**Data procurement:** The dataset is streamed directly from HuggingFace using the
`datasets` library (`load_dataset(..., streaming=True)`). No bulk download is required.
The pipeline reads only the rows needed for the stratified sample and discards the rest.
A `HF_TOKEN` environment variable can be set in `.env` for authenticated access (higher
rate limits), but is not required for this public dataset.

**Quantity and structure:** The full dataset contains approximately 4.1 million rows
across multiple sub-collections (`dataset_id`). The pipeline operates on a stratified
sample (see [Processed Data](#processed-data)).

#### Data Catalogue

: Data catalogue for Nikity/Epstein-Files. {#tbl-data-catalogue}

| # | Column | Type | Values / Range | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `dataset_id` | string | e.g. `"epstein_court_documents"` | Identifies the sub-collection (stratum) within the dataset |
| 2 | `doc_id` | string | e.g. `"doc_00042"` | Unique identifier for the source document |
| 3 | `file_name` | string | e.g. `"deposition_2019-07-31.pdf"` | Original filename |
| 4 | `file_type` | string | `"pdf"` or `"image"` | Source file type; pipeline filters to `"pdf"` |
| 5 | `online_url` | string (URL) | `https://judiciary.house.gov/...` | Direct link to the original DOJ/House document |
| 6 | `text_content` | string | variable length, 0–500 000+ chars | Pre-extracted text (OCR for scanned PDFs; structured extraction for digital PDFs) |
| 7 | `metadata` | string (JSON) | varies by sub-collection | Additional document metadata (date, author, classification level) — not used in this pipeline version |

---

## EDA Results

The plots below were generated by `eda/02_eda.py` from the stratified sample
(see [Processed Data](#processed-data) for sampling details). All plots use the pipeline's
dark theme (`#0D1117` background) for visual consistency with the frontend.

### Document Count per Sub-collection

![Document count per dataset_id in the stratified sample.](pics/docs_per_dataset.png){#fig-docs-per-dataset}

@fig-docs-per-dataset shows how many qualifying PDF documents were collected per stratum.
Where a stratum bar reaches exactly `SAMPLE_SIZE` (1 000), the stratum was fully filled.
Shorter bars indicate strata with fewer than 1 000 qualifying PDF documents — the pipeline
collects all available documents rather than discarding the stratum.

### File Type Distribution

![File type distribution in the sample (filtered to PDF).](pics/file_type_dist.png){#fig-file-type}

@fig-file-type shows the file type distribution in the sample. Because the pipeline
filters to `file_type == "pdf"`, only PDF documents appear here. The original corpus
also contains `"image"` documents (scanned pages not yet OCR-processed or stored as raw
images); these are excluded because their `text_content` is either null or empty, making
entity extraction impossible without a separate OCR step.

### Text Length Distribution

![Distribution of text length (characters) across sampled documents.](pics/text_length_hist.png){#fig-text-length}

@fig-text-length shows the character length distribution of `text_content` across all
sampled documents on a log-y scale (heavy right tail). Most documents are short-to-medium
length (a few thousand characters), consistent with letter-format legal documents.
A long right tail of very large documents corresponds to multi-page court transcripts and
deposition records.

**OCR quality note:** Variation in text length partly reflects OCR quality rather than
actual document length. Scanned PDFs with poor scan quality may produce very short
(nearly empty) text extracts. The `MIN_OCCURRENCES` filter in the entity extraction step
partially mitigates this by discarding entities that appear in very few documents (which
tend to be OCR artefacts).

### Largest Documents

![Top 20 documents by text content length.](pics/top20_largest_docs.png){#fig-top20}

@fig-top20 identifies the 20 largest documents by character count. These are typically
multi-session deposition transcripts or large court filings. Very long documents
disproportionately contribute to co-occurrence edges (more entity pairs per document),
which is a known limitation of document-level co-occurrence analysis.

### Coverage and Completeness

The dataset does not document how its coverage relates to the full DOJ corpus. It is
possible that some released documents are missing from the HuggingFace dataset, either
due to processing failures or collection decisions by the dataset authors. The
`online_url` field allows manual verification of individual documents, but a systematic
completeness assessment is outside the scope of this project.

---

## Processed Data {#processed-data}

### Overview

: Overview of processed datasets. {#tbl-processed-overview}

| Name | Source | Storage |
|---|---|---|
| Stratified sample | `Nikity/Epstein-Files` (filtered + sampled) | `eda/cache/sample.parquet` |
| Entity nodes | Derived from sample via spaCy NER | `eda/cache/nodes.parquet` / `nodes.json` |
| Co-occurrence edges | Derived from sample via co-occurrence counting | `eda/cache/edges.parquet` / `edges.json` |

### Stratified Sample (`sample.parquet`)

**Sampling strategy:** The pipeline keeps the first `SAMPLE_SIZE` qualifying documents
per `dataset_id`. "Qualifying" means `file_type == "pdf"` and `text_content` is a
non-empty, non-whitespace string.

**Rationale:** Stratified sampling by `dataset_id` ensures each sub-collection is
represented proportionally rather than allowing large strata to dominate. A purely random
sample from the full 4.1 M-row corpus would likely undersample small but potentially
important sub-collections (e.g. financial records, deposition transcripts).

**Schema:** same columns as the raw dataset (see @tbl-data-catalogue), but limited to
qualifying PDF rows.

**Configuring sample size:** `SAMPLE_SIZE` is defined in `eda/config.py`. Increasing it
(e.g. to 5 000) will improve entity coverage at the cost of longer streaming and NER time.

### Entity Nodes and Edges

**Extraction** (`eda/03_extract_entities.py`): spaCy `en_core_web_sm` NER extracts
`PERSON` and `ORG` entities from `text_content`. Entities are normalised (lowercase,
strip, collapse whitespace, trim punctuation artefacts) and filtered to those appearing
in at least `MIN_OCCURRENCES` (= 3) documents.

Install the model once with:
```bash
uv run python -m spacy download en_core_web_sm
```

**Co-occurrence edges:** Two entities share an edge if they appear in the same document.
Edge weight = number of shared documents. Edges below `MIN_EDGE_WEIGHT` (= 2) are
discarded.

**Limitations:**
- Entity resolution is **not** performed. "epstein", "jeffrey epstein", and "mr. epstein"
  are treated as three distinct nodes. This is the primary analytical limitation.
- `en_core_web_sm` has lower precision on OCR-noisy text than on clean newswire text.
  NER recall is reduced for entities in poorly scanned documents.
- Document-level co-occurrence over-estimates connection strength for very long documents.

### Network Visualisation

![Epstein Files co-occurrence network. Node size = betweenness centrality, node colour = Louvain community.](pics/network.png){#fig-network}

@fig-network shows the co-occurrence network derived from the stratified sample.
Node size encodes betweenness centrality (bridge actors are large). Node colour
encodes community membership (Louvain algorithm). Edge width encodes co-occurrence
weight. Only the top 30 nodes by betweenness are labelled to reduce clutter.

The largest connected component is used for all metrics. The full export
(`nodes.json`, `edges.json`) includes all nodes and edges for the interactive frontend.
